section.title: The Burning Bush
section.sectionRationale: One long call narrative unified by the theophany at the bush and Moses' five successive objections, each answered by God, until Aaron is appointed as spokesman in 4:14-17. This is the seam between Moses' private exile and his public mission.
section.thesis: The God who sees His people's affliction reveals His covenant name, I AM, and sends a reluctant, self-doubting shepherd to confront the most powerful throne on earth, proving that the mission rests on who God is, not on who Moses is.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: the bush that burns but is not consumed; God's commission and self-revelation of His name; Moses' objections and God's signs; the naming of Aaron as spokesman.

units.label: The bush that is not consumed

verses.text: Meanwhile, Moses was shepherding the flock of his father-in-law Jethro, the priest of Midian. He led the flock to the far side of the wilderness and came to Horeb, the mountain of God.
There the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a blazing fire from within a bush. Moses saw the bush ablaze with fire, but it was not consumed.
So Moses thought, “I must go over and see this marvelous sight. Why is the bush not burning up?”
When the LORD saw that he had gone over to look, God called out to him from within the bush, “Moses, Moses!” “Here I am,” he answered.
“Do not come any closer,” God said. “Take off your sandals, for the place where you are standing is {{qodesh|holy ground}}.”
Then He said, “I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.” At this, Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look at God.

units.label: I have seen, I have heard, I will send you

verses.text: The LORD said, “I have indeed seen the affliction of My people in Egypt. I have heard them crying out because of their oppressors, and I am aware of their sufferings.

I have come down to rescue them from the hand of the Egyptians and to bring them up out of that land to a good and spacious land, a land flowing with milk and honey, the home of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites.
And now the cry of the Israelites has reached Me, and I have seen how severely the Egyptians are oppressing them.
Therefore, go! I am sending you to Pharaoh to bring My people the Israelites out of Egypt.”
But Moses asked God, “Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh and bring the Israelites out of Egypt?”
“I will surely be with you,” God said, “and this will be the sign to you that I have sent you: When you have brought the people out of Egypt, all of you will worship God on this mountain.”
Then Moses asked God, “Suppose I go to the Israelites and say to them, ‘The God of your fathers has sent me to you,’ and they ask me, ‘What is His name?’ What should I tell them?”
God said to Moses, “{{ehyeh|I AM WHO I AM}}. This is what you are to say to the Israelites: ‘I AM has sent me to you.’”

God also told Moses, “Say to the Israelites, ‘The {{yhwh|LORD}}, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.’ This is My name forever, and this is how I am to be remembered in every generation.

Go, assemble the elders of Israel and say to them, ‘The LORD, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, has appeared to me and said: I have surely attended to you and have seen what has been done to you in Egypt.
And I have promised to bring you up out of your affliction in Egypt, into the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Hivites, and Jebusites, a land flowing with milk and honey.’

The elders of Israel will listen to what you say, and you must go with them to the king of Egypt and tell him, ‘The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. Now please let us take a three-day journey into the wilderness, so that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God.’
But I know that the king of Egypt will not allow you to go unless a mighty hand compels him.
So I will stretch out My hand and strike the Egyptians with all the wonders I will perform among them. And after that, he will release you.
And I will grant this people such favor in the sight of the Egyptians that when you leave, you will not go away empty-handed.
Every woman shall ask her neighbor and any woman staying in her house for silver and gold jewelry and clothing, and you will put them on your sons and daughters. So you will plunder the Egyptians.”

units.label: Signs for the doubting shepherd
units.note: staff, hand, and water; Aaron as spokesman

verses.text: Then Moses answered, “What if they do not believe me or listen to my voice? For they may say, ‘The LORD has not appeared to you.’”
And the LORD asked him, “What is that in your hand?” “A staff,” he replied.
“Throw it on the ground,” said the LORD. So Moses threw it on the ground, and it became a snake, and he ran from it.
“Stretch out your hand and grab it by the tail,” the LORD said to Moses, who reached out his hand and caught the snake, and it turned back into a staff in his hand.
“This is so that they may believe that the LORD, the God of their fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has appeared to you.”
Furthermore, the LORD said to Moses, “Put your hand inside your cloak.” So he put his hand inside his cloak, and when he took it out, his hand was leprous, white as snow.
“Put your hand back inside your cloak,” said the LORD. So Moses put his hand back inside his cloak, and when he took it out, it was restored, like the rest of his skin.
And the LORD said, “If they refuse to believe you or heed the witness of the first sign, they may believe that of the second.

But if they do not believe even these two signs or listen to your voice, take some water from the Nile and pour it on the dry ground. Then the water you take from the Nile will become blood on the ground.”
“Please, Lord,” Moses replied, “I have never been eloquent, neither in the past nor since You have spoken to Your servant, for I am slow of speech and tongue.”
And the LORD said to him, “Who gave man his mouth? Or who makes the mute or the deaf, the sighted or the blind? Is it not I, the LORD?
Now go! I will help you as you speak, and I will teach you what to say.”
But Moses replied, “Please, Lord, send someone else.”

Then the anger of the LORD burned against Moses, and He said, “Is not Aaron the Levite your brother? I know that he can speak well, and he is now on his way to meet you. When he sees you, he will be glad in his heart.
You are to speak to him and put the words in his mouth. I will help both of you to speak, and I will teach you what to do.
He will speak to the people for you. He will be your spokesman, and it will be as if you were God to him.”
But take this staff in your hand so you can perform signs with it.”

qodesh.gloss: Holy, set apart. The ground itself is not magical, but it is set apart by God's presence, the first time the word appears in Scripture attached to a physical place, anticipating tabernacle and temple.

ehyeh.gloss: I AM WHO I AM, or I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE. From the verb hayah, to be. God declares Himself self-existent, dependent on nothing, utterly reliable, and present with His people in whatever the moment requires.

yhwh.gloss: The LORD, God's personal covenant name, built from the same root as ehyeh, I AM. Traditionally read aloud as Adonai out of reverence, it is God's name forever, the name by which He is to be remembered in every generation.

basic.context: After forty years as a shepherd in Midian, Moses meets God at a bush that burns without burning up. God tells him He has seen Israel's suffering and is sending Moses to confront Pharaoh and lead the people out of Egypt. Moses objects five times, and God patiently answers each objection, finally allowing Aaron to speak for him.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real historical encounter, not a symbol or myth. The heart of the passage is God's self-revelation of His name and character, and it teaches that God's call does not depend on the qualifications of the one He calls, but on who He is.
basic.originalLanguages: God announces His name as Ehyeh asher ehyeh, I AM WHO I AM, built on the Hebrew verb to be. From this comes YHWH, the LORD, His personal covenant name forever. He is self-existent and utterly reliable, the God who simply is, and who will be present with His people however the moment requires.
basic.typology: The bush that burns yet is not consumed pictures God's holy presence dwelling with His people without destroying them, a pattern fulfilled when the Word became flesh and dwelt among sinners without being consumed by their sin, and when the Spirit indwells believers as a holy, undestroying fire.
basic.christ: Jesus takes the divine name for Himself, saying before Abraham was, I AM, John 8:58, provoking His hearers precisely because they understood the claim. He is the true and greater deliverer, sent not merely to bring people out of Egypt but out of slavery to sin, and He, unlike Moses, never once refuses the mission the Father gives Him.
basic.god: God is self-existent, compassionate, and covenant-keeping. He sees affliction, hears crying, and comes down to rescue, and He reveals His eternal name precisely at the moment He commits to act in history for His people.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 7:30-34 retells this scene in Stephen's speech. John 8:58 has Jesus claiming the I AM. Matthew 22:32 has Jesus citing verse 6 to prove the resurrection, since God is the God of the living.
basic.oneStory: This is the ignition point of the exodus: the God who kept His covenant reveals His eternal name and commissions a reluctant deliverer. It anticipates the whole redemptive pattern fulfilled in Christ, the great I AM who comes down to rescue His people from a bondage deeper than Egypt's.

context.literary: A classic prophetic call narrative: divine appearance, commission, objection, reassurance, sign, repeated objection, until the call is finally accepted, here stretched across five rounds of Moses' resistance before Aaron is appointed.
context.historical: Set at Horeb, also called Sinai, in the wilderness where Moses had been shepherding for decades; the passage assumes Israel's ongoing oppression in Egypt described in chapters 1 and 2 and looks ahead to the same mountain as the site of covenant giving in chapter 19.
context.canonical: The theological center of the whole book: God's revelation of His covenant name here becomes the name used throughout the rest of the Old Testament, and the promise of bringing Israel to this same mountain to worship is fulfilled in chapter 19.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal God's covenant name and character as the ground of Israel's coming deliverance, and to show that God equips whom He calls despite their sense of inadequacy.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a unique, unrepeatable theophany and call. It is not a template for how God always calls people today, but it does teach the enduring pattern that God's presence, not our competence, qualifies us for what He assigns.

debates.question: What exactly does the divine name I AM WHO I AM mean?

views.label: Absolute self-existence
views.case: God is declaring His eternal, uncaused being, dependent on nothing, the ground of all other existence.

views.label: I will be what I will be, active presence
views.case: The Hebrew can also carry a future sense, God promising to be present and active for His people however the moment requires, not merely a metaphysical statement.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the name grounds Israel's confidence: whether emphasizing being or active presence, God is declaring Himself utterly reliable and personally engaged in their deliverance.

study.originalLanguages: The name Ehyeh asher ehyeh comes from hayah, to be, giving Israel YHWH, the LORD, God's personal covenant name forever. The ground is qodesh, holy, set apart by God's presence rather than any inherent property, the pattern later filled out in tabernacle and temple.

typology.type: The bush that burns but is not consumed
typology.fulfillment: God's holy presence dwelling among His people without destroying them, fulfilled in the incarnation, John 1:14, and in the indwelling Spirit, 1 Corinthians 3:16.

typology.type: The reluctant deliverer sent to confront a hostile power
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Deliverer, willingly obeys the Father's commission without hesitation, Philippians 2:8, succeeding where Moses' initial reluctance is overcome only by God's patient persistence.

study.christ: Jesus applies the divine name to Himself directly, before Abraham was, I AM, John 8:58, and His hearers understood the claim well enough to try to stone Him. He is the fulfillment of the pattern begun here: the one sent by the great I AM to accomplish a deliverance far greater than the exodus, freeing captives from sin and death rather than from Pharaoh, Luke 4:18.
study.god: God is self-existent, unchanging, and utterly reliable, the meaning carried in His name. He is also intimately attentive: He has seen, heard, and known Israel's affliction, and He comes down personally to act. His patience with Moses' five objections shows a God who calls the reluctant and equips them rather than waiting for the qualified.

crossReferences.note: Stephen retells the burning bush encounter in his defense before the Sanhedrin.
Jesus applies the divine name I AM to Himself, provoking His hearers' outrage.
Jesus cites Exodus 3:6 to argue for the resurrection, since God is the God of the living, not the dead.
Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever, echoing the unchanging I AM.

study.oneStory: This is where the exodus is set in motion: God reveals His eternal, covenant-keeping name and sends a reluctant shepherd to confront the most powerful throne on earth. It anticipates the far greater deliverance accomplished by Christ, the true I AM, who came down not merely to see affliction but to bear it Himself for His people.

sermon.title: 'I Am Who I Am'
sermon.why: Piper works directly through Exodus 3:13-15, unpacking the divine name I AM WHO I AM and the name YHWH built from it, showing why God's absolute, self-existent being is the foundation for everything else Scripture reveals about Him. A clear, orthodox exposition of this exact passage.

quiz.q: What was significant about the bush Moses saw at Horeb?

options.text: It was ablaze with fire yet was not consumed, marking God's holy presence.
options.note: Verse 2 records this detail, and it draws Moses to turn aside and see, leading to God's call from within it.

options.text: It was simply an ordinary desert fire.
options.note: The text emphasizes its extraordinary nature, a fire that does not consume.

options.text: Moses set the bush on fire himself.
options.note: The fire and the voice both originate from God, not from Moses.

options.text: It had burned to ashes when Moses arrived.
options.note: The remarkable detail is precisely that it was not consumed despite burning.

quiz.q: What name does God reveal to Moses when asked what he should tell the Israelites?

options.text: The God of the Egyptians.
options.note: This contradicts the passage; God identifies Himself as the God of Israel's fathers, not of Egypt.

options.text: No name is given in this passage.
options.note: The name I AM WHO I AM and the LORD are both explicitly given.

options.text: God Almighty, the name He used with Abraham.
options.note: That name, El Shaddai, is referenced later in Exodus 6:3, but here God reveals the new, deeper name I AM.

options.text: I AM WHO I AM, from which comes the name the LORD, YHWH.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 record this revelation directly, God's self-existent, covenant name forever.

quiz.q: How many times did Moses object to God's commission, and how did God respond?

options.text: Moses objected once and God refused to answer.
options.note: God patiently answers every one of Moses' repeated objections with reassurance and signs.

options.text: Moses raised repeated objections about his worthiness, credibility, and speaking ability, and God patiently answered each with reassurance and signs.
options.note: Moses objects in 3:11, 3:13, 4:1, and 4:10, and God answers each, finally appointing Aaron in 4:14-16.

options.text: God rejected Moses for objecting and chose someone else entirely.
options.note: God grows angry at 4:14 but still sends Moses, pairing him with Aaron rather than replacing him.

options.text: Moses accepted the call immediately without hesitation.
options.note: The text records the opposite; Moses raises multiple objections before finally going.

quiz.q: What three signs did God give Moses to authenticate his calling?

options.text: Splitting the sea, providing manna, and giving the law.
options.note: Those events come much later in the exodus story, not as signs given here at the burning bush.

options.text: Healing the sick and raising the dead.
options.note: These are not the signs recorded in this passage.

options.text: No signs were given to Moses.
options.note: Three specific signs are explicitly given in verses 4:2-9.

options.text: The staff turning into a snake, his hand becoming leprous and being restored, and water from the Nile turning to blood.
options.note: Verses 4:2-9 describe these three signs given for Israel to believe Moses was sent by God.

quiz.q: Why is God's self-revelation as I AM significant for the mission Moses is given?

options.text: It reveals that God's plans depend on Moses' eloquence.
options.note: God explicitly answers Moses' concern about eloquence by asserting His own sovereignty over human speech, verse 4:11.

options.text: It is simply a poetic title with no bearing on the mission.
options.note: The name is directly tied to the promise of deliverance and God's covenant faithfulness, not merely decorative language.

options.text: It means God is distant and uninvolved in the mission.
options.note: The whole passage shows God intimately involved, coming down to rescue and commissioning Moses personally.

options.text: It grounds Israel's deliverance in God's own unchanging, self-existent, covenant-keeping character rather than in Moses' ability.
options.note: The name assures Moses and Israel that the mission rests on who God is, not on human qualification.

questions: Moses stood on holy ground because of God's presence, not because of anything special about the dirt. Where in your ordinary week might God be present in a way you have not paused to notice?

God said, I AM WHO I AM, grounding Moses' mission in His own unchanging character. When you feel unqualified for what God is asking of you, how does resting in who God is, rather than who you are, change your confidence?
Moses raised objection after objection, and God patiently kept answering rather than giving up on him. Where do you need to bring your honest doubts to God rather than hiding them?
God said He had seen, heard, and known Israel's affliction long before He acted. What suffering in your life or in someone else's do you need to trust that God has truly seen and heard?
God provided Aaron to help Moses speak, showing that His call often comes with provision we did not expect. Where might God be providing help for a task you feel unequipped to do alone?

translationNotes.note: Ehyeh asher ehyeh can be rendered I AM WHO I AM or I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE, since the Hebrew imperfect can carry ongoing being or unfolding future action; both senses assure Israel of God's reliable, active presence.

The phrase rendered unless a mighty hand compels him is literally except by a mighty hand, which some read as God's hand of judgment forcing Pharaoh's release rather than Pharaoh acting under any hand but his own.

The staff becomes a nachash, an ordinary snake, in contrast to Aaron's staff, which becomes a tannin, a larger serpent or monster, before Pharaoh in Exodus 7:10, a distinct and more dramatic sign for that royal audience.
